coredata.dc:title: Preparation, characterization and oxygen sensing properties of luminescent carbon dots assembled mesoporous silica microspheres
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Li
Zhang, Haoran
Zhou, Xiaohua
Liu, Yingliang
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, our effort was focused on preparation and oxygen sensing of luminescence carbon dots (CDs) assembled hollow mesoporous silica microspheres (HMSMs) and mesoporous silica microspheres (MSMs). MSMs doped with CDs showed shorter response time and recovery time comparing with HMSMs doped with CDs. This feature can be attributed to ordered channel structure of mesoporous carrier which can promote the gas diffusion effectively. While HMSMs doped with CDs shows a higher oxygen quenching response and the degree of quenching reach 80.35%. The response time was determined to be about 7s and the emission intensities of the samples were effectively reduced as the concentration of oxygen increased. These results indicate that the system we have developed can be used for oxygen detection in wide concentration range and is especially accurate for very low oxygen concentrations. The obtained CDs grafted hollow mesoporous silica microspheres (HMSMs) and mesoporous silica microspheres (MSMs) samples appears to be a promising sensing material for environmental detection application and would also find applications in catalyst, electrode, or related fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen sensing
Luminescence carbon dots
Hollow mesoporous silica microspheres
Mesoporous silica microspheres

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of hollow mesoporous silica microspheres (HMSMs)
Loading of CDs into mesoporous silica microspheres

National Natural Science Foundations of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 51203053 , 51372091 ), the Foundation for High-level Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong Province , the Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province , and the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A series of small angle XRD patterns for samples (a) SMSMs, (b) CMSMs, (c) TMSMs, (d) CHMSMs and (e) THMSMs.
FESEM and TEM images of (a1, a2) SMSMs, (b1, b2) CMSMs, (c1, c2) TMSMs, (d1, d2) CHMSMs and (e1, e2) THMSMs.

Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and DFT pore size distribution curves of (a) SMSMs, (a-CDs) 5% CDs-SMSMs, (b) CMSMs, (b-CDs) 5% CDs-CMSMs, (c) TMSMs, (c-CDs) 5% CDs-TMSMs, (d) CHMSMs, (d-CDs) 5% CDs-CHMSMs and (e) THMSMs, (e-CDs) 5% CDs-THMSNs.
FTIR spectra for samples (a-CDs) 5% CDs-SMSMs, (b-CDs) 5% CDs-CMSMs, (c-CDs) 5% CDs-TMSMs, (d-CDs) 5% CDs-CHMSMs and (e-CDs) 5% CDs-THMSNs.
Excitation and emission spectra of samples (a-CDs) 5% CDs-SMSMs, (b-CDs) 5% CDs-CMSMs, (c-CDs) 5% CDs-TMSMs, (d-CDs) 5% CDs-CHMSMs and (e-CDs) 5% CDs-THMSNs.

Typical luminescence intensity-based Stern-Volmer plots for 5% CDs assembled samples (a-CDs) 5% CDs-SMSMs, (b-CDs) 5% CDs-CMSMs, (c-CDs) 5% CDs-TMSMs, (d-CDs) 5% CDs-CHMSMs and (e-CDs) 5% CDs-THMSNs. The solid lines presents the best fit using the two-site model Eq. (2); Emission spectra of 5% CDs-HMSMs measured at different oxygen volume fractions (λex =369nm).

Response time and relative intensity change and reproducibility for samples (a-CDs) 5% CDs-SMSMs, (b-CDs) 5% CDs-CMSMs, (c-CDs) 5% CDs-TMSMs, (d-CDs) 5% CDs-CHMSMs and (e-CDs) 5% CDs-THMSNs on switching between 100% nitrogen and 100% oxygen.
Textural data of 5% CDs assembly mesoporous materials.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SBET: BET specific surface area; V: pore volume; D: pore diameter.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Values of response (I0/I100), t↓, t↑, Stern-Volmer model and Demas model oxygen-quenching fitting parameters of samples (a-CDs) 5% CDs-SMSMs, (b-CDs) 5% CDs-CMSMs, (c-CDs) 5% CDs-TMSMs, (d-CDs) 5% CDs-CHMSMs and (e-CDs) 5% CDs-THMSNs.

Values of response (I0/I100), t↓, t↑ and the degree of quenching of samples (a-CDs) 5% CDs-SMSMs, (b-CDs) 5% CDs-CMSMs, (c-CDs) 5% CDs-TMSMs, (d-CDs) 5% CDs-CHMSMs and (e-CDs) 5% CDs-THMSNs measured over 3months at every 30days.

head.ce:title: Preparation, characterization and oxygen sensing properties of luminescent carbon dots assembled mesoporous silica microspheres

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, our effort was focused on preparation and oxygen sensing of luminescence carbon dots (CDs) assembled hollow mesoporous silica microspheres (HMSMs) and mesoporous silica microspheres (MSMs). MSMs doped with CDs showed shorter response time and recovery time comparing with HMSMs doped with CDs. This feature can be attributed to ordered channel structure of mesoporous carrier which can promote the gas diffusion effectively. While HMSMs doped with CDs shows a higher oxygen quenching response and the degree of quenching reach 80.35%. The response time was determined to be about 7s and the emission intensities of the samples were effectively reduced as the concentration of oxygen increased. These results indicate that the system we have developed can be used for oxygen detection in wide concentration range and is especially accurate for very low oxygen concentrations. The obtained CDs grafted hollow mesoporous silica microspheres (HMSMs) and mesoporous silica microspheres (MSMs) samples appears to be a promising sensing material for environmental detection application and would also find applications in catalyst, electrode, or related fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, researchers have proposed various materials especially glowing metal complexes for the sensing of oxygen [1]. While the limited practical application of glowing metal complexes was based on the high price, environment unfriendly and these synthesis processes are complicated and time-consumed. Such as the ruthenium(II) complex covalently assembled mesoporous MSU-3 silica [2] showed fast response to oxygen, while these ruthenium(II) complexes limited practical application. Comparing with traditional luminescent species, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) exhibit great potential in light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [3], biosensing and bioimaging [4], sensors [5], and others due to their nontoxic, biocompatible, excellent chemical and photostability as well as superior optical properties [6]. In general, we can define CDs as isotropical in shape while the dimensions should be lower than 10nm. Unlike the semi-conductor QDs containing heavy metals, CDs is generally not considered as a toxic element.

Among the mesoporous materials for loading of CDs, SBA-15 are particularly attractive due to the thicker wall, uniform pore size, large surface area and high chemical stability, which would allow direct incorporation into oxygen sensing [7]. Carbon dots in mesoporous materials are an efficient alternative for oxygen sensing because they have a high chemical and photostability with low production costs and low toxicity.

There are less reports on the study of the oxygen sensing properties of CDs-based hollow mesoporous silica microspheres (HMSMs) and mesoporous silica microspheres (MSMs). In particular, mesoporous silica, discovered by Mobil researchers [8], is seen as a potentially application in sensor because of its high surface, tunable pore size and volume, and well-defined surface properties for modification [9]. The studies on novel nanostructure particles, especially inorganic oxide (such as TiO2, SiO2 and Fe3O4) hollow microspheres [10], have been one of the most vibrant fields in materials science due to the thicker wall, large surface area and high chemical stability [11], which are excellent for sensor, catalysis, drug delivery, pH-responsive delivery carrier, bio-medical applications [12–20]. HMSMs and MSMs with mesoporous structures have attracted much attention due to their potential applications in sensor, confined-space catalysis, drug storage and release, etc. Schacht et al. first demonstrated the preparation of hollow mesoporous silica spheres by cooperative assembly of silica and surfactants at oil-water interface [21]. Studies on HMSMs and MSMs indicated that they have a sustained release property and a much higher CDs-loading capacity than conventional mesoporous silica, such as MCM-41 and SBA-15. HMSMs and MSMs are well suited as a structure that can contain individual photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles and metal particles with mesopores, and the pores are wide enough to permit facile diffusion of reactants and products. Therefore, it is expected that the photoluminescent carbon dots loaded into hollow mesoporous silica microspheres materials have a great potential for sensors and light-emitting diodes application. In an oxygen sensor, a high diffusion coefficient of gas is necessary for a rapid response, a highly locally effective quenching around the oxygen probe is useful for good sensitivity [22,23]. The materials HMSMs and MSMs doped with carbon dots should also have a potential for gas sensing application, because of ordered channel structure of mesoporous carrier can promote the gas diffusion effectively.

Herein, we offered a simple and novel method to prepare successfully HMSMs and MSMs. Firstly we prepared mesoporous silica nanoparticles by using templates, and then developed MSMs by hydrothermal method. Direct impregnation method was used to incorporate CDs into HMSMs and MSMs, and explore its oxygen sensing properties. HMSMs and MSMs doped with carbon dots are an irreplaceable materials in oxygen sensor fields due to their nontoxic, biocompatible, excellent chemical and photostability as well as superior optical properties.

Tetradecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (TTAB, 99%), Stearyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (STAB, 98%) were analytic purity, supplied by aladdin. Cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) was purchased from Shanghai Boao Biological Technology Co., Ltd., Styrene (St) purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd., α,α′-azodiisobutyramidine dihydrochloride (AIBA) was supplied by Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd., Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, K-30, Mw=58,000) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was supplied from Tianjin Chemicals Co., Ltd., Ethanol (99.7%, Guangdong Guanghua Technology Co., Ltd.,), Triethanolamine (TEA, ⩾78.0%, Shanghai Lingfeng Co., Ltd.,) were used without any purification. N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-ammonia propyl methyl dimethoxy silane (N-aminoethyl-3-ammonia propyl methyl dimethoxy silane, Silane Coupler KH-602, m.w.=206.358) was from Guangzhou Longkai Co., Ltd., Citric acid anhydrous (99.5%, AR) was purchased from Aladdin. De-ionized water was used throughout this work.

MSMs were prepared by using STAB, CTAB and TTAB as the structure-directing agent and TEA as the co-template under mild conditions. In a typical procedure, STAB, CTAB and TTAB, respectively, was completely dissolved in 9mL of ethanol and 66mL of deionized water with stirring for 30min at room temperature, and then 3.9mL Triethanolamine (TEA) was added in the solution stirring 30min. Then 3.6mL tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was added dropwise to the solution and stirred for 3h at 333K. Next, the reaction stands for 48h in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 383K. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was filtered by Buchner funnel, thoroughly washed with distilled water five times and ethanol one time, and dried at 333K in an oven. Finally, the powder was transferred to a corundum crucible and calcined at 823K in furnace for 6h to remove the surfactant (STAB, CTAB and TTAB), denoted as SMSMs, CMSMs and TMSMs, respectively.

Mono-dispersed PS microspheres were synthesized by dispersion polymerization using PVP as the dispersant. In a typical process, St (10.0g), PVP (3.5g), AIBA (0.41g) and deionized water (100mL) were charged into a 250mL round-bottomed flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer, an N2 inlet. This mixture was degassed with nitrogen at room temperature for about 60min, followed by heating to 343K water bath and stirred for 24h. Finally, The as-prepared PS microspheres were collected to a bottle for the next use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hollow mesoporous silica microspheres (HMSMs)
ce:section.ce:para: 6.55g CTAB or TTAB was completely dissolved in 200mL of absolute ethanol by stirring for 10min, and then 8mL of TEOS was added dropwise to the solution and stirred for 60min. Subsequently, 25g PS microspheres was added into the mixture. About 10min later, 5mL of NH4OH was added to the reaction mixture. The synthesis procedure was performed at 323K under vigorous stirring for 3h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was filtered by Buchner funnel, thoroughly washed with distilled water five times and ethanol one time to remove the residual TEOS and NH4OH, and dried at 333K in an oven. The powder was transferred to a corundum crucible and calcined at 823K in furnace for 6h to obtain the hollow mesoporous silica microspheres. The sample was prepared by the template CTAB denoted as CHMSMs, and the template TTAB denoted as THMSMs.

According to Ref. [24], 30mL Silane Coupler KH-602 were placed into a 100mL three-necked flask and degassed with nitrogen for 10min. Heat was applied till the temperature reached 513K, 1.5g anhydrous citric acid was quickly added to the solution with vigorous stirring for 3min. The final products were purified by precipitation with petroleum ether three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading of CDs into mesoporous silica microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: In the synthesis procedure, 0.4g surfactant removed hollow mesoporous silica microspheres was immersed in 30mL ethanol, 5% CDs was added to the solution with stirring for 8h at room temperature. The precipitate was filtered by Buchner funnel, and dried at 333K in an oven. The final sample was marked as the following rule: 5wt% CDs grafted SMSMs is 5% CDs-SMSMs.

Infrared spectrum was recorded on Fourier spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere, using KBr as reference. The particle morphology was analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, XL-30; Philips, North Billerica, MA) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, TECNAI12, Holland). Nitrogen physisorption isotherms were measured using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020HD88 system. The pore size distributions were calculated from the DFT pore size distribution. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a monochromator (resolution: 0.2nm) and a 150W Xe lamp as the excitation source.

The oxygen-sensing properties based on luminescence intensity quenching of sample (such as 5% CDs-SMSMs) were characterized using the previously reported method [25]. Before the measurement, 0.2g powder samples were first pressed by a slide into pellets with 10mm in diameter and 1mm in thickness. For the Stern-Volmer plots measurements, oxygen and nitrogen were mixed at different concentrations via gas-flow controllers and flew directly into the gas chamber sealed with a closely fitting suba-seal rubber lid equipped with two (IN and OUT) tubes, and the results were recorded by the same Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

A series of small angle XRD patterns for samples (a) SMSMs, (b) CMSMs, (c) TMSMs, (d) CHMSMs and (e) THMSMs are shown in Fig. 1 . In the low angle region, a wide peak confirms ordered channel structure of samples (b, d and e). The structures and morphology of the samples were characterized by Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 , hollow mesoporous silica microspheres were successfully obtained with a spherical morphology and a hollow nanostucture. The highly monodispersed hollow mesoporous silica microspheres are obtained with particle size in the range between 251 and 271nm, the hollow nanostucture (such as the white areas in the black spheres) with sizes about 193–213nm in the shell, and the shell thickness of about 29nm. By further observations from the TEM image (Fig. 2a2–e2), there were mesoporous channels in the shells of hollow mesoporous silica microspheres, which was directed by (CTAB, TTAB) as the structure-directing agents. We found that the surfactants (STAB, CTAB, TTAB) as the structure-directing agents played a key role for the formation of mesoporous silica microspheres from the experimental results. As shown in Fig. 2, the mesoporous silica microspheres with the surfactants (STAB) as the structure-directing agent with small particle size. On closer inspection it does appear that the number of pores of silica microspheres gradually increased with the surfactants CTAB and the particle size with about 122nm in Fig. 2. It can be seen that the number of pores of silica microspheres further increased, and etched with about 20–45nm holes filling with microspheres (about 237nm), the microspheres was directed by TTAB as the structure-directing agent. More CDs could be grafted into mesoporous silica microspheres surface with holes filled with microspheres.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurement shows that MSMs and HMSMs exhibit a typical type IV isotherm, as expected for mesoporous silica microspheres with various pores in the shell [26]. The narrow pore size distribution calculated using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method from both the adsorption and desorption branches of the isotherm indicates that most of the pores have a diameter in the range of 0–60nm in Fig. 3 . Such a porous structure leads to a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area, the average value of the pore size and the pore volume are shown in Table 1 . The pore size becomes increasingly narrow after doping CDs, which is attributed to the confinement effect of CDs inside the channels of MSMs and HMSMs. The specific surface area and pore volume gradually decreased with CDs assembled in Table 1, because of the tunnel was filled with CDs.

To further confirm the mechanism of loading of CDs into mesoporous silica microspheres, Fourier transformation infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy is recorded and shown as Fig. 4 . For the samples (a-CDs, b-CDs, c-CDs, d-CDs and e-CDs), its FT-IR spectrum with several characteristic bands peaking around 1088cm−1, 1084cm−1, 1100cm−1, 1101cm−1 (νas , SiO), 801cm−1, 803cm−1, 804cm−1 (νs , SiO), 466cm−1, 468cm−1 (δ, SiOSi), which can be attributed to the vibrations of SiOSi bands. The wide vibration peak around 3440cm−1 is assigned to ν (SiOH) and ν (OH) in absorbed water, 1634cm−1 for δ (OH) in water. The vibration peaks (1517–1685cm−1) confirm the successful construction of CDs, which derived from amide group (CONR) in CDs [7]. (ν
= stretching vibration, δ =bending vibration, s =symmetric vibration, as =antisymmetric vibration).

Emission spectra of 5% CDs assembled mesoporous silica microspheres are shown in Fig. 5 . Fluorescence intensity of samples (a-CDs, b-CDs, c-CDs) was measured with 366nm as the excitation wavelength and 454nm as the emission wavelength at room temperature, the excitation wavelength for other samples (374nm d-CDs and 369nm e-CDs) and the emission wavelength is 454nm and 465nm, respectively. The wavelength range of excitation light was from 230nm to 435nm in the experiment. The maximum emission peak intensity value was found in the sample d-CDs. We can found that samples (SMSMs, CMSMs, CHMSMs and THMSMs) are well suited as a structure that can contain more carbon dots with mesopores, and the pores are wide enough to permit facile diffusion of oxygen. While the lowest emission peak intensity value is sample TMSMs because of the smaller CDs assembled, about 20–45nm holes filling with the microspheres.

Oxygen sensing performance of CDs doped MSMs and HMSMs is evaluated by their emission response to various oxygen concentrations. The emission spectrum of sample e-CDs measured at different oxygen concentrations at room temperature in the upper half of Fig. 6 . Emission shape and peak position of these composite samples is slightly change at different oxygen volume fraction. However, the emission intensities of the samples decreased obviously with increasing oxygen concentrations, indicating that their emission is quenchable by oxygen molecules. The degree of quenching for samples (a-CDs, b-CDs, c-CDs, d-CDs, e-CDs and f-CDs) reach 64.29%, 64.03%, 73.75%, 78.68% and 80.35%, respectively. These results showed that the CDs assembled mesoporous materials MSMs and HMSMs had a great potential for oxygen sensing application.

In homogeneous solution without complicating factors, luminophore quenching is described by the Stern-Volmer (SV) equation:
(1)
I
0
/ I =
τ
0
/ τ = 1 +
K
sv
P
O 2
= 1 + k
τ
0
P
O 2
where I and τ are the luminescence intensity and the excited-state lifetime of the related decay profile, respectively, the subscript 0 denotes the absence of oxygen, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, k is the bimolecular rate constant describing the efficiency of the collisional encounters between the luminophore and the quencher, and P O2 is the O2 concentration. An ideal Stern-Volmer curve is supposed to be a linear one with slope of Ksv, which, however, is not the case for Stern-Volmer curves shown in the bottom half of Fig. 6.

To explain this non-liner working curve, it is reasonable to assume that the light-emitting molecule is unevenly distributed in the pore channels of microspheres, which are distributed over two different sites. Luminescence molecules in different sites, characterized by different degrees of oxygen quenching, which result in nolinear Stern-Volmer response curve [27–30]. Assuming that there are two or more sites in our materials, only one of them is sensitive to oxygen, the others are not, in this case, the linear response Eq. (1) is not enough to fit the Stern-Volmer plots because of their downward curvature. Several models have been proposed to explain such nonlinearity, and the downward oxygen sensing Stern-Volmer plots can be well fitted by the Demas two-site model. The straight-line intensity Stern-Volmer Eq. (1) can be derived as two-site model equation:
(2)
I
0
I
=
1
f
01
1 +
K
SV 1
p
o 2
+
f
02
1 +
K
SV 2
p
o 2
here, f01 and f02 denote the fractional contributions of the total intensity from the luminescence molecules located at two different sites that exhibit two discrete Stern–Volmer constants K SV1 and K SV2, respectively.

Response and recovery character of our materials is evaluated by monitoring their emission intensity of the sample drops quickly with the increasing of oxygen concentration, and recovers when 100% nitrogen replacing oxygen, as shown in Fig. 7 . It is clear that the emission maximum is well preserved in pure nitrogen atmosphere, showing no obvious photobleaching. For discussion convenience, response time is defined as the time required for the luminescent intensity to decrease by 95% on changing from 100% nitrogen to 100% oxygen, i.e. t↓ (95%, N2→O2), similarly, and recovery time, i.e. t↑ (95% O2→N2), is defined as the time required for the luminescent intensity to reach 95% of its emission maximum when atmosphere is switched from 100% oxygen to 100% nitrogen. The higher values of Ksv and the lower values of f01 for sample (e-CDs) show a higher oxygen quenching response in Table 2 . The recovery time, response time and stability are also very important factors in evaluating oxygen sensor property. From Fig. 7, the baseline signal of the mesoporous materials doped with carbon dots was quite stable and reversible, there was also a rapidly response. For discussion convenience, maximum sensitivity is defined as I0/I100, where I0 and I100 are emission intensity values in pure nitrogen and pure oxygen atmosphere, respectively. The key sensing parameters I0/I100, t↓ and t↑ parameters are summarized in Table 2.

The sample b-CDs showed shorter response time t↓ (about 4.85s) and recovery time t↑ (about 23.23s) comparing with other samples. This feature can be attributed to certainly ordered channel structure of mesoporous carrier which can promote the gas diffusion effectively. The sample e-CDs shows a higher oxygen quenching response for the higher values of Ksv and the lower values of f01 and the degree of quenching reach 80.35%, while it shows longer response time t↓ (about 7.95s) and recovery time t↑ (about 32.56s) than the sample b-CDs. The most probable reasons for this higher performance of gas sensing can be attributed to the unique morphology that the hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (HMSNs) with ordered cylindrical pores in the shell, the higher crystallinity degree, smaller particle size and larger specific surface areas and private more space for target gases molecules. In addition, in order to detect the response of water vapor to samples, we dried gases (nitrogen and oxygen) with a desiccant and ran the same test of the sensing properties which resulted in only slightly varied experimental data. During our experimental processes, long-term stability of these samples was also closely monitored. Their response stability was measured over 3months at every 30days in Table 3 , and these results revealed that the effect of aging time on the average O2 sensing response stability is small enough to be neglected. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the response (I0/I) was 1.3%, which is below the accuracy (±4%) of the Hitachi-F7000 spectrophotometer. The sustained high response and excellent long-term stability arise from the increased flexibility that overcomes the pore shrinkage and/or collapse with time due to the covalent bond between the silica and the carbon dots.

Hollow mesoporous silica microspheres (HMSMs) and mesoporous silica microspheres (MSMs) were prepared in this experiment. HMSMs and MSMs doped with carbon dots are an irreplaceable materials in oxygen sensor fields due to their nontoxic, biocompatible, excellent chemical and photostability as well as superior optical properties. We found that the as-prepared samples had a reversible oxygen sensing signal and a good photochemical stability. MSMs doped with CDs (b-CDs) showed shorter response time t↓ (about 4.85s) and recovery time t↑ (about 23.23s) comparing with other samples. This feature can be attributed to certainly ordered channel structure of mesoporous carrier which can promote the gas diffusion effectively. HMSMs doped with CDs (e-CDs) shows a higher oxygen quenching response for the higher values of Ksv and the lower values of f01 and the degree of quenching reach 80.35%, while it showed longer response time and recovery time than the sample b-CDs. The most probable reasons for this higher performance of gas sensing can be attributed to the unique morphology that the hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (HMSNs) with ordered cylindrical pores in the shell, the higher crystallinity degree, smaller particle size and larger specific surface areas and private more space for target gases molecules. The emission intensity of the samples drops quickly with the increasing of oxygen concentration, and recovers when 100% nitrogen replacing oxygen. The resulting response signal of samples was stable and reversible during the test (over 3months). These results indicate that the system we have developed can be used for oxygen detection in the whole range and is especially accurate for very low oxygen concentrations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Grant Nos. 51203053, 51372091), the Foundation for High-level Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong Province, the Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province, and the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of oxygen concentrations by luminescence quenching of a polymer immobilized transition metal complex

Platinum (II) porphyrin-containing thermoresponsive poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) copolymer as fluorescence dual oxygen and temperature sensor
Nanostructured oxygen sensor-using micelles to incorporate a hydrophobic platinum porphyrin

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Wang received her BSc of Chemical Engineering and Technology from Wuyi University, Jiangmen, China in 2014, and then she began her Master of Applied Chemistry student experience under the supervising of Prof. Bingfu Lei since 2014 in SCAU. Her current research is the synthesis and characterization of nanoporous organic-inorganic hybrid materials for optical sensing.

Haoran Zhang received her MSc in 2004 in Inorganic Chemistry from Northeast Normal University in China, and then she received her PhD in 2011 in Jinan University (JNU). Then she has been working as a lectorship at SCAU. Her current research is synthesis and application of organic-inorganic hybrid compound used for sensing and display.

Xiaohua Zhou received her BSc of Analytical Chemistry from Jilin University in 1992, and MSc in 1995 in Inorganic Chemistry from the same university. She has worked as research scholar and chemistry faculty in SCAU since 1995. Now she is an associate professor and her main research interests are concerned with nucleic fluorescent probes, nanostructured microspheres, as well as drug controlled release carrier.

Yingliang Liu received his MSc degree in Chemistry in 1989 from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry of Chinese Academy of Sciences, and PhD in 1994 in Inorganic Chemistry from Sun Yat-Sen University. He has been a professor at the JNU since 2002 and moved from JNU to SCAU at 2012. He has been working on the topics including luminescence materials, functional glass and photoelectron materials.

Bingfu Lei received his BSc in 2001 in Food and Science Engineering and MSc in 2004 in Inorganic Chemistry from (Jinan University) JNU, and PhD in 2007 in Condensed Matter Physics from Changchun Institute of Optics Fine Mechanics and Physics of Chinese Academy of Sciences, and then he became a JSPS Postdoctoral Fellowship for Foreign Researchers at the Center for Advanced Science and Innovation of Osaka University from 2007 to 2009. He obtained a professor position at South China Agricultural University (SCAU) since 2012. His current research interests mainly focuses on the exploration of covalently grafted hybrid materials as gas sensors, chemosensors and high luminescent display devices. He also interests in the research of spectral converting and electrical-optical converting luminescent materials that using in agricultural field aiming at improving the light quality for plant photosynthesis.
